lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market vessel operators and ship-chartering businesses with crew that provide recurring deep-sea cargo transportation to or from foreign ports for external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Coastal, Great Lakes, and inland freight; passenger and cruise operations; freight forwarders and non-vessel-operating intermediaries; ports and terminals; ship managers without freight-transportation revenue; captive fleets; single-vessel financing or passive owning shells; and operators outside the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Voyage charters, time charters, liner service contracts, tariffs, and spot bookings paid by shippers, charterers, or logistics intermediaries for international ocean carriage; repeated trade lanes and customer shipping programs create recurring demand despite volatile rates.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and duties across NAICS 483000 water transportation, including inland and passenger operations, rather than NAICS 483111 or AI task exposure. I map the documented vessel-operation, maintenance, safety, and chain-of-command duties to low substitution exposure, while assigning higher exposure to chartering, documentation, voyage planning, compliance, billing, customer communication, procurement, and shore-based coordination. The broader population may include more small-vessel manual work than deep-sea freight and therefore may understate the target industry's shore-side exposure.
a.rationale: Deep-sea freight combines difficult-to-automate shipboard navigation, engineering, maintenance, cargo safety, and emergency work with information-heavy shore-side workflows. AI can reduce work in bills of lading, charter-party review, voyage and fuel planning, sanctions and compliance screening, claims triage, invoice audit, customer updates, and maintenance analytics, but current crew work remains predominantly physical and safety accountable.
a.change_evidence: Wage and hour data split between shipboard and shore-side roles at 15-20 LMM operators, with task-level time studies for chartering, operations, documentation, finance, compliance, and technical management, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: BLS does not publish a 483111-specific occupation table in the cited page.
Exposure is a task mapping, not an observed substitution rate.
Crewing models differ between U.S.-flag and foreign-flag vessels and between liner, tanker, and dry-bulk operations.

rho.rationale: Document and decision-support workflows can be implemented within five years, especially where data already arrive electronically. Realization is limited by legacy vessel and enterprise systems, connectivity, cyber risk, fragmented counterparties, auditability, safety cases, union and crew practices, and the need for experienced human review. IMO's new autonomous-ship framework is non-mandatory and retains master accountability, making wholesale shipboard labor removal unlikely within the horizon.
rho.change_evidence: Audited before-and-after deployments at comparable operators showing labor hours, exception and hallucination rates, safety outcomes, integration costs, cyber controls, and sustained five-year adoption would materially move this range.
caveats: No source directly measures five-year AI implementation for LMM deep-sea freight operators.
A small operator may use outsourced ship management, moving exposed labor outside its payroll.
International flag-state and port-state requirements create heterogeneous adoption constraints.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition directly covers external deep-sea cargo transportation and chartering with crew, so many operating firms fit the recurring outsourced-service lens. Eligibility is reduced by passive vessel-owning shells, captive fleets, entities without independently transferable contracts or operating capability, distressed one-vessel structures, and establishments controlled inside larger carrier groups.
e.change_evidence: Entity-level reconciliation of the 52 estimated LMM firms against vessel registries, beneficial ownership, charter contracts, operating certificates, related-party revenue, and audited EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
caveats: The official classification defines establishments, not transferable firms.
Shipping commonly uses one-purpose vessel entities that can inflate apparent firm counts.
The frozen n estimate is margin-bridged from receipts and may be sensitive to cyclical shipping margins.

s5.bridge: The cited $950 million Saltchuk acquisition of U.S.-flag liquid-bulk operator OSG proves that control transfers occur in the operating industry, but OSG is public and far above the LMM band. I use it only as evidence of strategic buyer appetite and apply a much lower bounded probability to eligible private LMM firms; the large-company example may overstate liquidity for small operators.
s5.rationale: Strategic consolidation, fleet renewal needs, owner succession, and cyclical distress create transfer opportunities, while vessel financing, environmental liabilities, charter concentration, flag restrictions, and volatile asset values can delay or reshape sales. A moderate five-year probability is defensible, but there is no denominator-based LMM transaction series.
s5.change_evidence: A five- to ten-year transaction census from VesselsValue, Clarksons, Capital IQ, Coast Guard and flag registries, and beneficial-ownership records, matched to the frozen LMM firm denominator and supplemented with owner-age interviews, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The observed transaction is much larger than the screened band.
A vessel sale is not necessarily a qualifying operating-company control transfer.
Private transactions and ownership changes in single-purpose vessel entities are incompletely disclosed.

q.bridge: FMC reports 384,811 original ocean service contracts and 996,803 amendments in FY2025, with more reliance on amendments and some volume redirected to spot markets. These counts cover regulated international liner contracting, including carriers larger than the lens, and do not measure retention of AI savings. I infer that contract periods delay pass-through while frequent amendments, spot exposure, and competitive repricing share benefits with customers; large-carrier contracting could overstate bargaining power for LMM firms.
q.rationale: Some implemented benefits are retained during a charter or service-contract term, and scarce vessel capacity can support pricing. However, deep-sea freight is globally contestable and rate-volatile; voyage tenders, charter renewals, broker transparency, fuel and bunker adjustments, capacity cycles, and spot pricing should transfer a material share of durable cost improvements to shippers.
q.change_evidence: A sample of target-company charter parties and service contracts, renewal and amendment histories, spot-versus-contract revenue, bunker and cost escalation clauses, customer concentration, and realized margins after prior productivity changes would materially change the estimate.
caveats: FMC service-contract statistics primarily illuminate liner trades and not all dry-bulk, tanker, or project-cargo charters.
Contract counts do not reveal rate terms, duration, or customer power.
Freight-rate volatility may dominate modest operating savings.

d5.bridge: UNCTAD forecasts global seaborne trade volume to average 2% annual growth from 2026 through 2030, which compounds to about 1.104 over five years. The source covers global trade rather than U.S.-industry operators, and route, commodity, sanctions, and flag exposure can diverge sharply. I use 1.10 as the base while widening the downside and upside for the screened U.S. firm population.
d5.rationale: International merchandise, energy, mineral, and agricultural flows continue to require ocean carriage, and the cited forecast implies moderate volume growth. Trade policy, geopolitical rerouting, commodity cycles, overcapacity, and customer or route concentration can produce contraction for individual LMM operators even when global tonnage grows.
d5.change_evidence: Target-weighted five-year cargo forecasts by route and commodity, contracted backlog, charterer shipment plans, fleet supply and scrapping schedules, and downside scenarios for tariffs and sanctions would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Global seaborne volume is not U.S. operator demand and does not control for service quality.
Ton-miles can diverge from tons when routes change.
The forecast predates some possible trade, conflict, and policy changes during the horizon.

o.rationale: Almost all surviving year-five ocean cargo still requires an accountable carrier or vessel operator to provide seaworthy assets, navigation, maintenance, compliance, insurance, contracting, and emergency response. Autonomous technology can relocate or reduce crew work, but IMO's 2026 framework retains a master with overall responsibility and fully crewless ships remain limited, so software does not eliminate the operator role.
o.change_evidence: Commercial deployment records for autonomous deep-sea cargo ships, approved safety cases, crew complements, remote-operations staffing, insurer requirements, and shipper willingness to contract directly with autonomy vendors would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The estimate concerns the continued need for an operator, not preservation of current onboard headcount.
The MASS Code is new, non-mandatory, and may evolve before the end of the horizon.
Consolidation could move service to a different operator without eliminating operator-required demand.

narrative.executive_view: Deep-sea freight has meaningful AI opportunity in documentation, chartering, planning, compliance, and shore-side operations, but its low frozen labor share and safety-critical shipboard work limit the direct labor prize. Demand remains physical and operator-required, while globally transparent and volatile pricing constrains long-run retention.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can draft and reconcile shipping documents, review charter parties, optimize voyages and fuel, screen sanctions and compliance records, triage claims, audit invoices, summarize vessel telemetry, and assist maintenance planning. Navigation, engineering, cargo safety, maintenance, and emergencies remain physical, regulated, and accountable to qualified people.
narrative.value_capture: Time charters and service contracts can preserve savings until amendment or renewal, but the large spot market, broker and index visibility, global capacity cycles, and competitive tenders make cost advantages contestable. Retention will vary materially by niche, customer concentration, route scarcity, and contract duration.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen dataset estimates 52 firms in the EBITDA band, but the classification can contain passive or single-purpose vessel entities and subsidiaries that are not independently transferable operating companies. Strategic control transfers are visible, yet the available example is far larger than the target band and does not establish a small-firm turnover rate.
narrative.demand_durability: UNCTAD expects moderate global seaborne-trade growth through 2030, and physical cargo cannot be converted into software. Route-specific trade policy, war, sanctions, commodity shifts, and fleet oversupply still make individual-company demand and economics much more volatile than the global forecast.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are rate and asset-price cycles, customer and charter concentration, fuel and emissions costs, cyber exposure, sanctions, liability, financing, and costly fleet renewal. Evidence is weakest on the eligible-firm denominator, LMM transfer frequency, task-level labor mix, and the actual sharing of productivity gains through private charters.
narrative.principal_driver: Information-heavy shore-side workflows sit beside durable physical demand that still requires an accountable operator.
narrative.principal_weakness: A 15.78% frozen labor share and competitive, volatile freight pricing limit the economic leverage and retention of AI savings.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 483000 Water Transportation
sources.supports: Provides the all-water-transport occupation structure and confirms the published population covers employers of all sizes in NAICS 483000, including both deep-sea/coastal/Great Lakes and inland transport.

sources.title: Water Transportation Workers: Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.supports: Documents vessel operation, maintenance, safety, physical skills, long tours, training, and 84,300 water-transport jobs in 2024 with 1% projected growth through 2034.

sources.title: Review of Maritime Transport 2025, Chapter I: International Maritime Trade
sources.supports: Forecasts total seaborne trade to grow at an annual average of 2% from 2026-2030 and containerized trade by 2.3%.

sources.title: IMO adopts first global Code for autonomous ships
sources.supports: Reports that fully crewless or remote ships remain limited; the non-mandatory MASS Code applies from July 2026, requires safety and approval controls, and retains master responsibility even off ship.

sources.title: Federal Maritime Commission FY 2025 Annual Report
sources.supports: Reports 384,811 original service contracts and 996,803 amendments in FY2025 and says shippers relied more on amendments while redirecting some volume to the spot market.

sources.title: Overseas Shipholding Group Enters Into a Definitive Agreement to Be Acquired by Saltchuk Resources
sources.supports: Documents a 2024 agreed control transaction for a U.S.-flag liquid-bulk transportation operator at $950 million total transaction value.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 483111 Deep Sea Freight Transportation
sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments providing deep-sea cargo transportation to or from foreign ports and includes deep-sea ship chartering with crew.
